words.definition: to officially end or get rid of something
examples: The school board voted to abolish the old dress code.
Many countries have worked to abolish unfair laws.

words.definition: extremely unreasonable or silly
examples: It would be absurd to wear a winter coat in the desert.
The clown's absurd antics made everyone laugh.

words.definition: to treat someone or something badly or wrongly
examples: No one should abuse animals or people.
The coach warned players not to abuse their power on the field.

words.definition: the ability to enter, use, or reach something
examples: Students need a password to access the online library.
The ramp gives wheelchair users access to the building.

words.definition: to succeed in doing something
examples: She worked hard to accomplish her goal of reading fifty books.
The team accomplished a lot during the science fair.

words.definition: something important that you succeed in doing
examples: Graduating fifth grade is a big achievement.
Winning the spelling bee was her proudest achievement.

words.definition: ready to attack or argue; forceful
examples: The aggressive dog barked at strangers.
He played an aggressive style of basketball.

words.definition: to switch back and forth between two things
examples: We alternate between math and reading each morning.
The traffic lights alternate between red and green.

words.definition: the height of something above sea level
examples: Pilots must know the plane's altitude at all times.
Mountain climbers feel the cold more at high altitude.

words.definition: a character who opposes the main character
examples: The villain is the antagonist in most adventure stories.
In the play, the antagonist tried to stop the hero.

words.definition: a word that means the opposite of another word
examples: 'Hot' is an antonym of 'cold.'
We made a list of antonyms for our vocabulary homework.

words.definition: worried or nervous about something
examples: She felt anxious before her first piano recital.
The anxious puppy hid under the bed during the storm.

words.definition: easy to see or understand; obvious
examples: It was apparent that she had studied for the test.
The crack in the wall was apparent after the earthquake.

words.definition: close to an exact amount but not exact
examples: The approximate cost of the trip was two hundred dollars.
Can you give me an approximate time of arrival?

words.definition: a pleasant smell
examples: The aroma of fresh bread filled the kitchen.
We enjoyed the sweet aroma of the blooming flowers.

words.definition: to believe something is true without proof
examples: Don't assume everyone agrees with you.
I assumed the store was open, but it was closed.

words.definition: to surprise someone very much
examples: The magician's tricks astound the audience.
Her talent on the violin will astound you.

words.definition: able to be used or obtained
examples: There are three computers available in the lab.
Is the librarian available to help us?

words.definition: a large mass of snow sliding down a mountain
examples: The skiers heard an avalanche rumble in the distance.
Heavy snow can cause a dangerous avalanche.

words.definition: a large formal meal for many people
examples: The school held a banquet to honor the volunteers.
Guests dressed up for the wedding banquet.

words.definition: a drink, especially one other than water
examples: Hot chocolate is my favorite winter beverage.
Please choose a beverage from the menu.

words.definition: lacking strong flavor or interest
examples: The soup tasted bland without any salt.
The movie had a bland plot with no surprises.

words.definition: a severe snowstorm with strong winds
examples: School was canceled because of the blizzard.
Drivers stayed home during the fierce blizzard.

words.definition: to move slightly or make something move
examples: The heavy box would not budge an inch.
Try to budge the stuck door with your shoulder.

words.definition: to do something badly or clumsily
examples: He tried to fix the bike but bungled the repair.
Don't bungle your chance to make a good first impression.

words.definition: in a careful way that avoids danger
examples: The cat cautiously stepped onto the wet floor.
We cautiously crossed the icy sidewalk.

words.definition: something difficult that tests your ability
examples: Climbing the rock wall was a fun challenge.
She accepted the challenge to run a mile every day.

words.definition: a person in a story; also, the qualities that make someone who they are
examples: The main character in the book is very brave.
Honesty is an important part of good character.

words.definition: to join or mix things together
examples: Let's combine our ideas for the group project.
The recipe tells you to combine flour and sugar.

words.definition: a person or animal that spends time with you
examples: Her dog is a loyal companion on long walks.
He traveled with a companion who shared his love of hiking.

words.definition: to want something very much
examples: After running, I crave a cold glass of water.
She began to crave adventure beyond her small town.

words.definition: sympathy and concern for others who are suffering
examples: The nurse showed compassion to every patient.
We should treat others with kindness and compassion.

words.definition: to make up for something lost or to pay someone
examples: Extra rest can compensate for a poor night's sleep.
The company will compensate workers for their overtime.

words.definition: to follow a rule, order, or request
examples: All students must comply with the safety rules.
The factory agreed to comply with the new regulations.

words.definition: to create something, such as music or writing
examples: Mozart began to compose music at a young age.
Take a moment to compose your thoughts before speaking.

words.definition: an idea or general notion
examples: Gravity is a difficult concept to understand at first.
The concept of fairness is important in games.

words.definition: feeling sure of yourself and your abilities
examples: She felt confident before the math competition.
Practice helps you become a confident reader.

words.definition: to change something into a different form
examples: You can convert inches to centimeters using a chart.
The old warehouse was converted into apartments.

words.definition: a path or direction; also, a class or part of a meal
examples: The river changed course after the flood.
We signed up for a science course next semester.

words.definition: polite and respectful toward others
examples: The courteous waiter thanked us for dining there.
Always be courteous when you meet new people.

words.definition: a discussion in which people argue different sides of an issue
examples: The class held a debate about recycling.
The candidates will debate on television tonight.

words.definition: to become smaller, weaker, or less; to refuse politely
examples: Sales began to decline after the holidays.
She had to decline the invitation because she was busy.

words.definition: to give time or effort to something important
examples: Athletes dedicate hours to training every week.
The park was dedicated to a local hero.

words.definition: to take something away from someone
examples: Lack of sleep can deprive you of energy.
No one should deprive children of an education.

words.definition: to discover or notice something
examples: The smoke alarm can detect fire quickly.
Scientists detect changes in the weather with special tools.

words.definition: to say words aloud for someone to write down; to give orders
examples: The teacher will dictate spelling words for the test.
You should not let fear dictate your decisions.

words.definition: a written or printed paper that gives information
examples: Please sign the document before you leave.
Historians study old documents to learn about the past.

words.definition: an exact copy of something
examples: Keep a duplicate key in case you lose the first one.
The printer made a duplicate of the original photo.

words.definition: safe to eat
examples: Berries in the forest are not always edible.
The cake was decorated with edible flowers.

words.definition: to put someone or something in danger
examples: Pollution can endanger wildlife in the river.
Speeding endangers everyone on the road.

words.definition: to become greater, worse, or more serious
examples: The argument began to escalate quickly.
Officials tried to stop the conflict from escalating.

words.definition: to escape or avoid something
examples: The rabbit tried to evade the fox.
He attempted to evade answering the difficult question.

words.definition: to make someone very annoyed or frustrated
examples: The delay will exasperate passengers waiting at the gate.
His constant interruptions exasperate the teacher.

words.definition: to dig out earth to uncover something
examples: Archaeologists excavate ancient ruins carefully.
Workers will excavate the site before building begins.

words.definition: to use physical or mental effort
examples: You must exert force to push the heavy cart.
She had to exert all her concentration on the test.

words.definition: to show something publicly; a display
examples: The museum will exhibit dinosaur fossils this summer.
Each student created an exhibit for the science fair.

words.definition: to show great joy or triumph
examples: Fans exulted when their team won the championship.
She exulted after receiving her award.

words.definition: weak and lacking strength
examples: The feeble light barely lit the dark hallway.
After being sick, he felt too feeble to play outside.

words.definition: extremely cold
examples: The frigid wind made us hurry indoors.
Polar bears survive in frigid Arctic temperatures.

words.definition: extremely large
examples: A gigantic whale surfaced near the boat.
They built a gigantic sandcastle on the beach.

words.definition: a deep, narrow valley with steep sides
examples: The hikers looked down into the rocky gorge.
A river runs through the bottom of the gorge.

words.definition: a person who protects or cares for someone
examples: Her aunt became her legal guardian.
The lifeguard acts as a guardian of swimmers' safety.

words.definition: not clear because of mist, smoke, or lack of detail
examples: The mountains looked hazy in the morning fog.
My memory of that day is hazy.

words.definition: enthusiastic, energetic, or large and satisfying
examples: We enjoyed a hearty breakfast before the hike.
The team gave a hearty cheer after the win.

words.definition: a word that sounds the same as another but has a different meaning
examples: 'Bear' and 'bare' are homonyms.
Our teacher asked us to find homonyms in the poem.

words.definition: exactly the same in every way
examples: The twins wore identical shirts to school.
These two keys look identical.

words.definition: to light up or make clear
examples: Streetlights illuminate the road at night.
The teacher used examples to illuminate a tricky math problem.

words.definition: extremely large or great
examples: The stadium has immense seating capacity.
She felt immense relief when the test was over.

words.definition: causing admiration because of skill or quality
examples: Her impressive speech won first place.
The ancient temple is an impressive sight.

words.definition: free from control by others; able to do things alone
examples: She is an independent thinker who forms her own opinions.
The country became independent after many years of struggle.

words.definition: working hard and steadily
examples: The industrious ants carried food all day.
He is an industrious student who always finishes homework early.

words.definition: very strong or extreme
examples: The intense heat made it hard to play outside.
She felt intense excitement before the performance.

words.definition: to stop or catch something before it reaches its destination
examples: The defender tried to intercept the pass.
Spy agencies intercept secret messages.

words.definition: great happiness and celebration
examples: There was jubilation in the streets after the victory.
The crowd erupted in jubilation when the final buzzer sounded.

words.definition: one's family or relatives
examples: All her kin gathered for the reunion.
He traveled far to visit his kin.

words.definition: very comfortable, elegant, and expensive
examples: They stayed in a luxurious hotel by the ocean.
The cat stretched out on the luxurious blanket.

words.definition: important, serious, or larger in size
examples: Pollution is a major problem in big cities.
She chose biology as her major subject in college.

words.definition: very small; a tiny copy of something
examples: She collects miniature horses for her dollhouse.
The artist painted a miniature portrait.

words.definition: small or not very important
examples: It was only a minor scratch on the bike.
The team made minor changes to the plan.

words.definition: playful trouble or harmless naughtiness
examples: The puppy got into mischief while we were away.
The children were full of mischief on April Fools' Day.

words.definition: a king, queen, or other supreme ruler
examples: The monarch ruled the kingdom for decades.
Butterflies are sometimes called monarchs because of their orange wings.

words.definition: concerned with right and wrong behavior
examples: The story teaches an important moral about honesty.
She made a moral choice even when it was difficult.

words.definition: a traditional story, often explaining natural events; something untrue
examples: Greek myths tell stories of gods and heroes.
It is a myth that bats are blind.

words.definition: the person who tells a story
examples: The narrator described the setting at the start of the book.
In the movie, the narrator guides us through the plot.

words.definition: to find the way through or across an area
examples: Sailors navigate using maps and stars.
It can be hard to navigate a new school on the first day.

words.definition: bad, harmful, or expressing denial
examples: Try not to focus on negative thoughts before a test.
The answer to 'Is it raining?' can be negative: 'No.'

words.definition: calm and not showing much interest or worry
examples: He gave a nonchalant shrug when asked about the score.
She tried to look nonchalant even though she was nervous.

words.definition: many; existing in large numbers
examples: Numerous birds gathered at the feeder.
She has read numerous books about space.

words.definition: a fertile spot in a desert where water is found
examples: Travelers rested at the oasis under the palm trees.
The park felt like an oasis in the busy city.

words.definition: no longer used because something newer exists
examples: Typewriters are mostly obsolete today.
That computer model became obsolete within a few years.

words.definition: a particular time when something happens; a special event
examples: We dressed up for the special occasion.
On that occasion, she told her funniest joke.

words.definition: to remove a leader or government by force
examples: Rebels tried to overthrow the dictator.
The revolution overthrew the old regime.

words.definition: to forgive; an official release from punishment
examples: Please pardon the mess in the classroom.
The governor may pardon someone who was wrongly convicted.

words.definition: land covered with grass where animals graze
examples: Cows wandered across the green pasture.
The farmer fenced the pasture to keep the sheep safe.

words.definition: a person walking rather than traveling in a vehicle
examples: Pedestrians should use the crosswalk.
The city built wider sidewalks for pedestrians.

words.definition: to die, especially in a sudden or violent way
examples: Many plants will perish without water.
Food left in the sun can perish quickly.

words.definition: to turn to stone; to make someone very frightened
examples: Legend says the gorgon could petrify people with one look.
The loud crash seemed to petrify the little dog.

words.definition: easy to carry or move
examples: She brought a portable speaker to the picnic.
The laptop is small and portable.

words.definition: a word part added to the beginning of a word to change its meaning
examples: 'Un-' is a prefix meaning 'not,' as in 'unhappy.'
Learning prefixes helps you understand new words.

words.definition: to keep something in its original state; to protect
examples: We must preserve forests for future generations.
Jam is made to preserve fruit for months.

words.definition: the main character in a story
examples: The protagonist faces many challenges in the novel.
Readers often root for the protagonist to succeed.

words.definition: to give or supply something needed
examples: Schools provide students with books and supplies.
Trees provide shade on hot summer days.

words.definition: to buy something
examples: We will purchase tickets online before the show.
She saved money to purchase a new bike.

words.definition: showing things as they really are; practical
examples: The painting looks very realistic.
Set realistic goals you can actually reach.

words.definition: to comfort someone and remove their fears
examples: The doctor reassured the patient that the test was normal.
Mom reassured me that everything would be fine.

words.definition: to rule as a king or queen; the period of rule
examples: Queen Elizabeth's reign lasted many years.
Peace reigned in the land after the treaty.

words.definition: able to be trusted or depended on
examples: She is a reliable friend who always keeps her promises.
We need a reliable car for long trips.

words.definition: to need something; to demand
examples: Plants require sunlight and water to grow.
The job will require strong math skills.

words.definition: to look or be like someone or something else
examples: She resembles her older sister.
Dolphins resemble fish but are actually mammals.

words.definition: to keep or continue to have something
examples: Try to retain what you learn by reviewing notes.
The sponge can retain a lot of water.

words.definition: to stop working, usually because of age
examples: My grandfather will retire next year.
Athletes often retire from sports in their thirties.

words.definition: to go back to a previous state or condition
examples: Without practice, skills can revert to what they were before.
The land may revert to forest if left alone.

words.definition: a way or path from one place to another
examples: We chose the scenic route through the mountains.
The bus route stops near our house.

words.definition: to walk in a slow, relaxed way
examples: We sauntered through the park on Sunday afternoon.
The cat likes to saunter across the sunny porch.

words.definition: not often; rarely
examples: It seldom snows in our town.
She seldom misses a day of practice.

words.definition: without meaning or purpose; lacking good judgment
examples: The senseless noise kept everyone awake.
Fighting over something small is senseless.

words.definition: to cut or break apart
examples: The storm severed the power lines.
He had to sever the rope to free the boat.

words.definition: to slide smoothly, like a snake
examples: The snake began to slither through the grass.
We watched the eel slither along the river bottom.

words.definition: slow and lacking energy
examples: I feel sluggish when I don't get enough sleep.
The sluggish river barely moved in the heat.

words.definition: to fly high in the air; to rise quickly
examples: Eagles soar above the cliffs.
Prices began to soar after the shortage.

words.definition: alone; done without others
examples: The solitary hiker enjoyed the quiet trail.
Tigers are mostly solitary animals.

words.definition: alone; a performance by one person
examples: She sang a solo at the concert.
He flew solo for the first time yesterday.

words.definition: thinly scattered; not dense
examples: Trees are sparse on the dry hillside.
Only sparse attendance was expected on the rainy day.

words.definition: a sudden burst of speed, effort, or liquid
examples: He ran in a spurt to catch the bus.
Water came out in a spurt from the hose.

words.definition: a careful plan to achieve a goal
examples: Our team developed a strategy to win the game.
Good study strategy includes reviewing notes daily.

words.definition: a word part added to the end of a word to change its meaning
examples: '-ful' is a suffix meaning 'full of,' as in 'helpful.'
Adding a suffix can turn a verb into a noun.

words.definition: to die or suffer from lack of air
examples: Never cover your face in a way that could suffocate you.
Smoke can suffocate people trapped in a fire.

words.definition: the highest point of a mountain; a meeting of leaders
examples: Climbers reached the summit at sunrise.
World leaders met at the summit to discuss climate change.

words.definition: to hang something; to stop something temporarily
examples: The bridge cables suspend the walkway above the river.
School may suspend a student who breaks serious rules.

words.definition: a word that means the same or nearly the same as another
examples: 'Happy' is a synonym of 'joyful.'
The thesaurus lists synonyms for each word.

words.definition: a sharp claw, especially of a bird of prey
examples: The eagle gripped the fish in its talons.
Hawks use their talons to catch small animals.

words.definition: to tease or provoke someone in a cruel way
examples: It is wrong to taunt classmates about their mistakes.
Rival fans taunted each other from the stands.

words.definition: careful about spending money; not wasteful
examples: My thrifty aunt always looks for sales.
Being thrifty helps you save for big purchases.

words.definition: to change words from one language into another
examples: Can you translate this sentence into Spanish?
Apps can translate signs when you travel abroad.

words.definition: relating to hot, humid regions near the equator
examples: Parrots live in tropical rainforests.
We dreamed of a tropical vacation on a sunny island.

words.definition: able to be seen
examples: The stars became visible after dark.
Make sure your name is visible on your homework.

words.definition: related to seeing or sight
examples: The visual effects in the movie were amazing.
Some learners prefer visual aids like charts and diagrams.

words.definition: producing strong, clear images in the mind; bright in color
examples: She gave a vivid description of the storm.
The vivid colors of the sunset painted the sky.

words.definition: a wild, natural area with few or no people
examples: They camped deep in the wilderness.
Bears roam freely in the northern wilderness.

words.definition: to pull back or remove; to take money from a bank
examples: Troops began to withdraw from the border.
You can withdraw cash from the ATM.

words.definition: to leave behind permanently
examples: The crew had to abandon the sinking ship.
Never abandon a pet when you move away.

words.definition: existing in large amounts
examples: Rainfall was abundant this spring.
The garden had an abundant harvest of tomatoes.

words.definition: the ability to enter, use, or reach something
examples: Students need a password to access the online library.
The ramp gives wheelchair users access to the building.

words.definition: to provide room or adjust to fit someone's needs
examples: The hotel can accommodate fifty guests.
Teachers accommodate different learning styles in class.

words.definition: to gather or collect over time
examples: Dust can accumulate on shelves if you don't clean.
She began to accumulate savings for college.

words.definition: to change to fit new conditions
examples: Animals adapt to survive in harsh climates.
We must adapt our plans when the weather changes.

words.definition: to stick firmly; to follow rules closely
examples: Glue helps labels adhere to bottles.
All members must adhere to the club rules.

words.definition: extreme physical or mental pain
examples: He cried out in agony after twisting his ankle.
Waiting for the results was agony.

words.definition: loyalty to a person, group, or country
examples: Citizens pledge allegiance to the flag.
Knights swore allegiance to their lord.

words.definition: a strong desire to achieve something
examples: Her ambition is to become a doctor.
Hard work turns ambition into success.

words.definition: more than enough; plentiful
examples: There was ample food for everyone at the picnic.
The room had ample space for a desk and bookshelf.

words.definition: severe pain or suffering, especially emotional
examples: She felt anguish when her pet was lost.
The letter expressed the writer's deep anguish.

words.definition: to expect or look forward to something
examples: We anticipate a busy week before the holiday.
Fans anticipate the release of the new book.

words.definition: worried or nervous about something
examples: She felt anxious before her first piano recital.
The anxious puppy hid under the bed during the storm.

words.definition: clothing
examples: The store sells athletic apparel.
Workers must wear safety apparel on the construction site.

words.definition: to be attractive or interesting; a request for help
examples: Bright colors appeal to young children.
The lawyer filed an appeal to overturn the decision.

words.definition: anxious or fearful about the future
examples: She felt apprehensive about starting a new school.
He was apprehensive before his first speech.

words.definition: very dry with little rainfall
examples: Cacti thrive in arid deserts.
The arid land could not support many crops.

words.definition: having an exaggerated sense of one's own importance
examples: The arrogant player refused to pass the ball.
Nobody likes an arrogant attitude.

words.definition: a feeling of wonder and respect, often mixed with fear
examples: We stared in awe at the towering waterfall.
The vast night sky filled her with awe.

words.definition: unable to produce plants or offspring; empty
examples: The barren field had no crops.
Nothing grew on the barren hillside.

words.definition: a guiding light or signal; a source of inspiration
examples: The lighthouse served as a beacon for ships.
She became a beacon of hope for her community.

words.definition: helpful or good for you
examples: Exercise is beneficial to your health.
Reading daily is beneficial for vocabulary growth.

words.definition: a careless mistake
examples: Sending the email to the wrong person was a blunder.
The general's blunder cost his army the battle.

words.definition: noisy, energetic, and cheerful
examples: The boisterous crowd cheered at the game.
Boisterous puppies tumbled across the yard.

words.definition: to refuse to buy or use something as a protest
examples: Consumers chose to boycott the unfair company.
Students organized a boycott of the cafeteria food.

words.definition: a heavy load; something difficult to bear
examples: Carrying the heavy backpack was a burden.
She did not want to burden her friends with her worries.

words.definition: an organized effort to achieve a goal
examples: The class ran a campaign to reduce litter.
Politicians launch campaigns before elections.

words.definition: the maximum amount something can hold or do
examples: The stadium has a capacity of ten thousand seats.
She has a great capacity for learning languages.

words.definition: the city where a government is located; wealth used to produce more wealth
examples: Washington, D.C., is the capital of the United States.
The business needed capital to open a second store.

words.definition: arranged in the order that events happened
examples: We listed the presidents in chronological order.
The timeline showed events in chronological sequence.

words.definition: related to a city or the duties of a citizen
examples: Voting is an important civic responsibility.
The civic center hosts community events.

words.definition: clearness in thought, expression, or appearance
examples: She explained the rules with clarity.
The clarity of the photograph was amazing.

words.definition: to work together with others
examples: Scientists collaborate across countries on research.
We will collaborate on the history project.

words.definition: to crash into each other with force
examples: Two cars collided at the intersection.
Planets may collide in space over billions of years.

words.definition: to praise or recommend someone
examples: The principal commended students for their kindness.
I commend you for trying your best.

words.definition: spoken or written remarks that explain or discuss something
examples: The sportscaster provided commentary during the game.
We wrote commentary about the poem in class.

words.definition: small and tightly packed; an agreement
examples: The compact car fit into the tiny parking space.
They signed a compact to share resources.

words.definition: calmness and self-control
examples: She kept her composure during the difficult interview.
Deep breaths helped him regain composure.

words.definition: giving a lot of information in few words
examples: Please write a concise summary of the chapter.
Her concise answer saved everyone time.

words.definition: permission or agreement
examples: You need a parent's consent to go on the trip.
All members gave consent to the new rule.

words.definition: a result of an action
examples: Every choice has a consequence.
Breaking the law can lead to serious consequences.

words.definition: to protect from waste or harm; to save
examples: We should conserve water during a drought.
Park rangers work to conserve wildlife habitats.

words.definition: easy to notice; standing out
examples: His bright red hat was conspicuous in the crowd.
There was a conspicuous stain on the shirt.

words.definition: continuing without stopping; unchanging
examples: The constant noise made it hard to concentrate.
Gravity provides a constant pull on objects.

words.definition: to make something dirty or impure
examples: Chemicals can contaminate drinking water.
Wash your hands so you don't contaminate the food.

words.definition: the words and situation surrounding something that help explain its meaning
examples: Use context clues to figure out unfamiliar words.
Historical context helps us understand old documents.

words.definition: happening without interruption
examples: Rain fell in a continuous drizzle all day.
The factory runs continuous shifts to meet demand.

words.definition: strong public disagreement about an issue
examples: The new law caused controversy across the country.
There was controversy over who won the debate.

words.definition: easy to use or suited to your needs
examples: Online ordering is convenient for busy families.
The store's location is convenient to our school.

words.definition: to deal successfully with a difficult situation
examples: She learned to cope with stress by exercising.
Communities must cope with floods and other disasters.

words.definition: warm and friendly
examples: They received a cordial welcome at the neighbor's house.
Despite their rivalry, the coaches were cordial after the game.

words.definition: to prepare land for crops; to develop a skill or relationship
examples: Farmers cultivate fields in the spring.
Reading helps cultivate a love of learning.

words.definition: growing by addition over time
examples: Your cumulative score includes all quiz results.
The cumulative effect of small savings adds up.

words.definition: to announce officially or clearly
examples: The judge will declare the winner.
Colonies declare independence with bold statements.

words.definition: a sudden flood; an overwhelming amount
examples: A deluge of rain flooded the streets.
She received a deluge of birthday messages.

words.definition: closely packed together; thick
examples: The dense forest blocked most sunlight.
Fog was so dense we could barely see the road.

words.definition: to use up or reduce greatly
examples: Long droughts deplete water supplies.
Running too many apps can deplete your phone battery.

words.definition: to put money in a bank; a layer of material left behind
examples: Please deposit your homework in the tray.
Minerals form deposit layers in rock over time.

words.definition: to choose or name for a specific purpose
examples: The teacher will designate a line leader each week.
This room is designated for quiet study.

words.definition: feeling hopeless or willing to do anything to change a bad situation
examples: They were desperate for help after the storm.
A desperate move saved the team in the final seconds.

words.definition: to become worse over time
examples: Old buildings deteriorate without repairs.
His health began to deteriorate after the injury.

words.definition: conversation between two or more people; speech in a story
examples: The play's dialogue sounded natural and funny.
Honest dialogue can resolve many conflicts.

words.definition: showing steady, careful effort
examples: She is a diligent student who checks her work twice.
Diligent practice leads to improvement.

words.definition: to become or make smaller or less
examples: The pain will diminish with rest.
Loud music can diminish your ability to hear warnings.

words.definition: the ability to make careful, wise choices
examples: Use discretion when sharing personal information online.
The manager handled the complaint with discretion.

words.definition: to disagree with an official opinion or decision
examples: One judge voiced dissent in the ruling.
Peaceful dissent is part of a free society.

words.definition: to mix into a liquid and disappear; to break up formally
examples: Sugar will dissolve in hot tea.
The club voted to dissolve at the end of the year.

words.definition: clearly different or separate
examples: Each species has distinct markings.
There are two distinct parts to the assignment.

words.definition: variety; the inclusion of different types of people or things
examples: Our class celebrates diversity of cultures and ideas.
Rainforests have great diversity of plant life.

words.definition: relating to the home or to one's own country
examples: Domestic chores include cooking and cleaning.
Domestic flights stay within the same country.

words.definition: to control or be the most important
examples: Tall trees dominate the landscape.
One team began to dominate the second half.

words.definition: extreme and sudden
examples: The drought required drastic water restrictions.
Doctors took drastic steps to save the patient.

words.definition: the length of time something lasts
examples: The duration of the movie is two hours.
Rest for the full duration of the recovery period.

words.definition: to live in a place; to think about something for a long time
examples: Many families dwell in apartments downtown.
Try not to dwell on small mistakes.

words.definition: when one object in space blocks the light of another
examples: We watched the solar eclipse with special glasses.
The moon can eclipse the sun during the day.

words.definition: the system of money, trade, and industry in a country
examples: A strong economy creates jobs.
Families plan their budget around the economy.

words.definition: strange and frightening in a mysterious way
examples: An eerie silence filled the empty hall.
Fog gave the forest an eerie glow.

words.definition: a change caused by something else
examples: The effect of exercise is better health.
What effect did the new rule have on behavior?

words.definition: achieving results without wasting time or energy
examples: LED bulbs are more efficient than old lights.
An efficient study plan saves time before tests.

words.definition: detailed and complicated; to add more detail
examples: She wore an elaborate costume to the play.
Please elaborate on your answer with examples.

words.definition: qualified or allowed to do something
examples: Students with good grades are eligible for the honor roll.
You must be eighteen to be eligible to vote.

words.definition: to escape from or avoid capture
examples: The fox managed to elude the hunters.
The answer eluded him until he reread the problem.

words.definition: to meet or experience unexpectedly
examples: Hikers may encounter bears in the woods.
We encountered a problem with the printer.

words.definition: equal in value, amount, or meaning
examples: One dollar is equivalent to one hundred cents.
'Big' and 'large' are equivalent in meaning.

words.definition: to burst out suddenly, as lava from a volcano
examples: The volcano may erupt without warning.
Laughter erupted when the comedian told a joke.

words.definition: respect and admiration
examples: Teachers hold students who try hard in high esteem.
Self-esteem grows when you achieve your goals.

words.definition: to develop gradually over time
examples: Languages evolve as people use them.
Species evolve to survive in changing environments.

words.definition: to make something seem larger or more important than it is
examples: Don't exaggerate how hard the test was.
Fish stories often exaggerate the size of the catch.

words.definition: to do extremely well at something
examples: She excels in math and science.
Athletes excel when they train consistently.

words.definition: to leave out or keep out
examples: The club should not exclude anyone because of differences.
The price excludes tax and shipping.

words.definition: a wide, open area
examples: We crossed a vast expanse of desert.
The expanse of ocean stretched to the horizon.

words.definition: to use unfairly for one's own benefit; to make full use of
examples: Companies must not exploit workers.
Explorers learned to exploit natural resources carefully.

words.definition: no longer existing
examples: Dinosaurs have been extinct for millions of years.
Some languages become extinct when no one speaks them.

words.definition: to remove or pull out
examples: Dentists extract teeth when they cannot be saved.
Scientists extract DNA from samples in the lab.

words.definition: something that contributes to a result
examples: Weather is a factor in crop growth.
Hard work was a key factor in her success.

words.definition: having been something in the past
examples: The former captain now coaches the team.
She visited her former school for the reunion.

words.definition: to create or develop carefully (third person singular)
examples: The chef formulates new recipes each season.
The committee formulates policies for the school.

words.definition: to join together into one
examples: Heat can fuse metal pieces in welding.
The communities fused into one larger town.

words.definition: useless; having no chance of success
examples: It is futile to argue with someone who won't listen.
Their futile search ended when night fell.

words.definition: to produce or create something
examples: Wind turbines generate electricity.
Brainstorming helps generate new ideas.

words.definition: a category of art, music, or literature
examples: Mystery is my favorite book genre.
Jazz is a popular music genre.

words.definition: the natural home of an animal or plant
examples: Polar bears live in an Arctic habitat.
Destroying forests removes wildlife habitat.

words.definition: dangerous
examples: Chemicals in the lab can be hazardous.
Driving in icy conditions is hazardous.

words.definition: a trick or deception
examples: The UFO sighting turned out to be a hoax.
Don't believe every online story—it might be a hoax.

words.definition: unfriendly; wanting to fight
examples: The hostile crowd booed the visiting team.
Desert conditions can be hostile to plants.

words.definition: a phrase whose meaning is different from the literal words
examples: 'Break a leg' is an idiom meaning 'good luck.'
Learning idioms helps you understand everyday speech.

words.definition: to set on fire; to spark interest or emotion
examples: A spark can ignite dry grass.
The speech ignited excitement in the crowd.

words.definition: extremely large or great
examples: The stadium has immense seating capacity.
She felt immense relief when the test was over.

words.definition: to create or perform without preparation
examples: Jazz musicians often improvise solos.
We had to improvise a tent when it started raining.

words.definition: not skilled or clumsy
examples: The inept juggler dropped all the balls.
He felt inept at first but improved with practice.

words.definition: certain to happen; unavoidable
examples: Change is inevitable as you grow older.
Rain seemed inevitable after those dark clouds appeared.

words.definition: the power to affect how someone thinks or acts
examples: Good friends have a positive influence on you.
Advertising can influence what people buy.

words.definition: clever and inventive
examples: She found an ingenious way to organize her desk.
The ingenious trap caught the mouse safely.

words.definition: a new idea, method, or invention
examples: Smartphones were a major innovation in communication.
Schools encourage innovation in science projects.

words.definition: to frighten or threaten someone
examples: Bullies should not intimidate classmates.
The size of the opponent did not intimidate her.

words.definition: cheerful and friendly
examples: The jovial host made everyone feel welcome.
His jovial laugh filled the room.

words.definition: a natural ability to do something well
examples: She has a knack for solving puzzles.
He has a knack for making people smile.

words.definition: freedom to act within limits
examples: The teacher gave us leeway on the due date.
Drivers need leeway when roads are slippery.

words.definition: laws made by a government body
examples: New legislation banned texting while driving.
Congress debates legislation before it becomes law.

words.definition: free time for relaxation
examples: Reading is my favorite leisure activity.
We spent the leisure hours playing board games.

words.definition: to set free
examples: Soldiers helped liberate the captured town.
Education can liberate people from poverty.

words.definition: similar appearance; a portrait
examples: The statue bears a strong likeness to the president.
Artists capture a person's likeness in a painting.

words.definition: to stay longer than expected
examples: Guests lingered after the party to talk.
The smell of cookies lingered in the kitchen.

words.definition: exactly as stated; not figurative
examples: The literal meaning of 'cold feet' is feet that are cold.
Take his warning in the literal sense.

words.definition: to dislike very much
examples: I loathe waking up before sunrise.
She loathes dishonesty in any form.

words.definition: to attract or tempt someone
examples: Bright colors lure fish toward the bait.
Advertisements lure shoppers with big sales.

words.definition: more than half of a group
examples: The majority of students voted for pizza.
A majority rules in democratic decisions.

words.definition: temporary and improvised
examples: They built a makeshift shelter from branches.
A cardboard box served as a makeshift table.

words.definition: to control or handle skillfully; sometimes unfairly
examples: You manipulate clay to shape a pot.
Do not manipulate others to get your way.

words.definition: to be amazed; something wonderful
examples: Visitors marvel at the ancient ruins.
The Grand Canyon is a natural marvel.

words.definition: very large and heavy
examples: A massive iceberg floated near the ship.
The project requires a massive amount of work.

words.definition: the greatest possible amount
examples: The maximum speed on this road is fifty-five.
Fill the container to the maximum line.

words.definition: small in amount; not enough
examples: They survived on a meager meal of bread and soup.
Meager rainfall hurt the farmers' crops.

words.definition: nothing more than; only
examples: It was a mere scratch, not a serious injury.
At mere twelve years old, she ran a 5K race.

words.definition: movement from one place to another, often seasonally
examples: Bird migration brings geese south in winter.
Human migration shaped many countries' histories.

words.definition: to copy someone's actions or sounds
examples: Parrots mimic human speech.
Children often mimic their parents' habits.

words.definition: extremely small; also a unit of time
examples: Scientists study minute organisms under microscopes.
Wait one minute before opening the oven.

words.definition: boring because it never changes
examples: The monotonous beeping kept us awake all night.
A monotonous job can feel tiring even if it is easy.

words.definition: to discuss to reach an agreement
examples: Countries negotiate treaties to avoid war.
We negotiated who would present first.

words.definition: a goal; not influenced by personal feelings
examples: Our objective is to finish the project on time.
Scientists try to remain objective in experiments.

words.definition: something that blocks progress
examples: Fear can be an obstacle to trying new things.
They cleared the obstacle from the trail.

words.definition: knowing everything
examples: An omniscient narrator knows all characters' thoughts.
No human is truly omniscient.

words.definition: the beginning of something
examples: At the onset of winter, birds fly south.
Medicine works best at the onset of symptoms.

words.definition: a person who expects good things to happen
examples: An optimist sees challenges as opportunities.
She is an optimist even when plans change.

words.definition: to begin or come from a particular place
examples: Coffee originates from Ethiopia.
The idea originated during our group discussion.

words.definition: requiring great care and effort
examples: Restoring the painting was painstaking work.
She took painstaking notes during the lecture.

words.definition: to restate something in different words
examples: Please paraphrase the paragraph in your own words.
He paraphrased the quote so it was easier to understand.

words.definition: a humorous imitation of something
examples: The skit was a parody of a famous movie.
Writers use parody to poke fun at trends.

words.definition: to treat someone cruelly because of who they are
examples: No group should persecute others for their beliefs.
History books describe regimes that persecuted citizens.

words.definition: to fall suddenly and steeply
examples: Temperatures can plummet overnight in winter.
Stock prices plummet during a crisis.

words.definition: to own or have something
examples: She possesses great talent for music.
The museum possesses rare ancient artifacts.

words.definition: the state of being very poor
examples: Charities work to reduce poverty worldwide.
The family escaped poverty through education.

words.definition: exact and accurate
examples: Measure the ingredients with precise amounts.
Give a precise answer instead of guessing.

words.definition: a difficult or unpleasant situation
examples: We were in a predicament when the car broke down.
Honesty can help you out of a predicament.

words.definition: to say what will happen before it does
examples: Meteorologists predict the weather using data.
Can you predict the ending of the story?

words.definition: an unfair opinion formed without enough knowledge
examples: We must fight prejudice and treat everyone fairly.
Prejudice can hurt people who deserve respect.

words.definition: coming before the main part; initial
examples: Preliminary results look promising.
The team held a preliminary meeting before the event.

words.definition: early; simple; from ancient times
examples: Primitive tools were made from stone.
Early computers seem primitive compared to today's.

words.definition: something treated as more important than others
examples: Safety is our top priority on field trips.
Finish homework before leisure activities is a good priority.

words.definition: important or well known; sticking out
examples: She is a prominent scientist in her field.
The prominent nose is a family trait.

words.definition: to drive or push forward
examples: Oars propel the boat across the lake.
Ambition can propel you toward your dreams.

words.definition: to bring someone to trial for a crime
examples: The state may prosecute those who break the law.
Lawyers prosecute cases in criminal court.

words.definition: to succeed and grow strong
examples: Businesses prosper when customers are happy.
Communities prosper when schools and jobs are strong.

words.definition: to cause a reaction, often anger
examples: Do not provoke the dog by pulling its tail.
His rude comment provoked an argument.

words.definition: to follow or chase; to work toward a goal
examples: Detectives pursue clues to solve crimes.
She will pursue a career in engineering.

words.definition: a long search for something important
examples: Knights went on a quest for the holy grail.
Her quest for knowledge led her to many libraries.

words.definition: to tell a story again; to count again
examples: She will recount her adventure to the class.
Officials recount votes when results are close.

words.definition: a safe place protected from danger
examples: Birds find refuge in the wetland preserve.
The library was a quiet refuge from the noisy hall.

words.definition: to strengthen or support
examples: Practice will reinforce what you learned.
Steel bars reinforce the concrete wall.

words.definition: unwilling or hesitant
examples: He was reluctant to try the new food.
She gave a reluctant yes to helping after school.

words.definition: deep regret for doing wrong
examples: He felt remorse after breaking his friend's toy.
A sincere apology shows remorse.

words.definition: far away; distant
examples: They live in a remote village in the mountains.
Use the remote to change the channel.

words.definition: firm and determined
examples: She remained resolute despite setbacks.
The resolute leader refused to give up.

words.definition: to hold back or control
examples: Officers restrain suspects safely during arrests.
Try to restrain your excitement until the results are final.

words.definition: to get revenge or strike back
examples: It is better not to retaliate when someone is rude.
The team retaliated with two quick goals.

words.definition: to get something back
examples: The dog retrieves the ball from the pond.
I need to retrieve my jacket from the locker.

words.definition: very strict, thorough, or demanding
examples: The training program is rigorous but effective.
Scientists use rigorous methods in experiments.

words.definition: relating to the countryside, not the city
examples: They grew up in a rural area with wide fields.
Rural schools sometimes have long bus routes.

words.definition: to save something from loss or damage
examples: Workers tried to salvage items from the flooded house.
We salvaged a good grade by studying hard the night before.

words.definition: a place of safety and protection
examples: The wildlife sanctuary protects endangered species.
Churches once served as sanctuary for those in need.

words.definition: a military attack that surrounds a place to force surrender
examples: The castle endured a long siege.
Supplies ran low during the siege.

words.definition: important or large enough to matter
examples: There was a significant improvement in her grades.
The discovery was significant for science.

words.definition: relating to the sun
examples: Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity.
A solar eclipse happens when the moon blocks the sun.

words.definition: to calm or relieve pain
examples: Soft music can soothe a crying baby.
Lotion helps soothe sunburned skin.

words.definition: not moving
examples: Remain stationary while the photo is taken.
The stationary bike is used for indoor exercise.

words.definition: to hold back or suppress; to make breathing difficult
examples: Don't stifle your creativity with too many rules.
Thick smoke can stifle people in a fire.

words.definition: to try hard to achieve something
examples: We strive to do our best every day.
Athletes strive for gold medals.

words.definition: lower in rank; less important
examples: A sergeant is subordinate to a captain.
Safety is never subordinate to speed on the job.

words.definition: coming after something else in time
examples: The first test was hard; subsequent ones were easier.
Subsequent chapters explain the experiment.

words.definition: higher in rank or quality
examples: This brand is superior in durability.
She reported the problem to her superior.

words.definition: something added to improve or complete
examples: Vitamins supplement a healthy diet.
We read a supplement to learn more about the topic.

words.definition: to move in a large group; a large group of insects
examples: Bees swarm around the hive in spring.
Tourists swarm to the famous landmark each summer.

words.definition: able to be touched; real and concrete
examples: The reward was tangible: a trophy and a medal.
Kindness is not always tangible, but it is real.

words.definition: to bring to an end
examples: The contract will terminate next month.
The referee may terminate the game for safety.

words.definition: the physical features of a piece of land
examples: Rocky terrain made hiking difficult.
Maps show terrain with hills, rivers, and valleys.

words.definition: a quality that makes one person or thing different from another
examples: Patience is a helpful trait in teachers.
Curiosity is a common trait among scientists.

words.definition: to change completely in form or appearance
examples: Caterpillars transform into butterflies.
Paint can transform a dull room.

words.definition: to carry people or goods from one place to another
examples: Trucks transport food to grocery stores.
Ferries transport cars across the river.

words.definition: dangerous and unpredictable; disloyal
examples: Icy roads are treacherous for drivers.
The treacherous villain betrayed his friends.

words.definition: in complete agreement by everyone
examples: The vote was unanimous to adopt the new rule.
The jury reached a unanimous decision.

words.definition: one of a kind; unlike anything else
examples: Each snowflake has a unique pattern.
Her unique voice made her stand out in the choir.

words.definition: hard to control; disorderly
examples: The unruly crowd pushed toward the stage.
Unruly hair stuck up in every direction.

words.definition: relating to a city
examples: Urban areas have many buildings and buses.
Urban gardens grow food on rooftops.

words.definition: to leave empty; to move out
examples: Please vacate your seat when the train arrives.
Tenants must vacate the apartment by Friday.

words.definition: a decision made by a jury in court
examples: The jury announced a guilty verdict.
Everyone waited nervously for the verdict.

words.definition: the edge; the point when something is about to happen
examples: Flowers bloom on the verge of spring.
She was on the verge of tears after the sad movie.

words.definition: full of energy and life; bright in color
examples: The market was vibrant with music and color.
Vibrant posters covered the classroom walls.

words.definition: absolutely necessary; essential for life
examples: Water is vital for all living things.
Sleep plays a vital role in learning.

words.definition: a serious promise
examples: Doctors take a vow to help patients.
She made a vow to read every day.
